Space is a near-vacuum; there’s no air, so sound can’t travel.
“Space” is commonly defined to begin at the Kármán line ~100 km (62 mi) above Earth.
The Sun contains about 99.8% of the solar system’s mass.
The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star powered by nuclear fusion (proton–proton chain).
Light from the Sun takes ~8 minutes 20 seconds to reach Earth.
A light-year is a distance: ~9.46 trillion km (~5.88 trillion miles).
Earth’s Moon is tidally locked, so the same face always points toward Earth.
The Moon is slowly receding from Earth at ~3.8 cm (1.5 in) per year.
The Moon’s “far side” isn’t always dark; it receives sunlight, too.
A lunar day (sunrise to sunrise) lasts about 29.5 Earth days.
Mercury’s year (~88 Earth days) is shorter than its day (one solar day ~176 Earth days).
Venus rotates “backwards” (retrograde rotation) relative to most planets.
Venus is the hottest planet due to a runaway greenhouse effect (~465°C / 869°F).
Mars has two small moons: Phobos and Deimos.
A day on Mars (a “sol”) is ~24 hours 39 minutes.
Mars has the largest volcano in the solar system: Olympus Mons.
Mars also hosts Valles Marineris, a canyon system over 4,000 km long.
The asteroid belt lies between Mars and Jupiter.
Ceres is the only dwarf planet in the inner solar system (in the asteroid belt).
Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system.
Jupiter’s Great Red Spot is a giant storm larger than Earth.
Jupiter has a faint ring system made of dust.
Saturn’s ring system is mostly ice with some rock and dust.
Saturn is less dense than water (its average density is <1 g/cm³).
Uranus rotates on its side with an axial tilt of ~98°.
Neptune has the fastest winds in the solar system (over 1,000 mph / 1,600 km/h measured).
Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt.
The Kuiper Belt is a region of icy bodies beyond Neptune.
The Oort Cloud is a distant, spherical reservoir of comets (hypothesized, not yet directly observed).
Comets often have two tails: a dust tail and an ion (gas) tail.
Meteoroid = small space rock; meteor = streak of light in the atmosphere; meteorite = rock that reaches the ground.
Dwarf planets officially recognized by the IAU include Ceres, Pluto, Haumea, Makemake, and Eris.
The International Space Station (ISS) has been continuously occupied since 2000.
The ISS orbits Earth roughly every 90 minutes.
Hubble Space Telescope (launched 1990) orbits Earth and observes in visible, UV, and near-IR light.
The James Webb Space Telescope (launched 2021) observes mainly in infrared and operates near the Sun–Earth L2 point.
Voyager 1 is the most distant human-made object; it entered interstellar space in 2012.
Voyager probes each carry a Golden Record with sounds and images of Earth.
New Horizons flew by Pluto in 2015, revealing a heart-shaped region (Tombaugh Regio).
New Horizons later flew by Arrokoth (2014 MU69) in the Kuiper Belt in 2019.
Earth’s magnetic field deflects much of the solar wind, creating the magnetosphere.
Auroras are caused by charged particles from the Sun interacting with Earth’s atmosphere.
Solar activity follows an ~11-year cycle of sunspot highs and lows.
Coronal mass ejections (CMEs) can disrupt satellites and power grids on Earth.
Lagrange points are gravitational balance points; spacecraft can “park” or orbit around them efficiently.
GPS satellites must account for relativity (time dilation) to maintain accuracy.
Rockets reach orbit by gaining horizontal speed, not just altitude.
Reentry heating comes from air compression and friction as spacecraft plow through the atmosphere.
The Heliopause marks the boundary where the solar wind is stopped by the interstellar medium.
Earth’s atmosphere thins gradually; there’s no sharp “edge,” just decreasing density.
The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy.
The Milky Way’s disk is ~100,000 light-years across (order of magnitude).
Our solar system orbits the Milky Way’s center roughly every 225–250 million years.
The nearest star to the Sun is Proxima Centauri (~4.24 light-years away).
Alpha Centauri is the nearest star system (triple system) to the Sun.
The Andromeda Galaxy (M31) is the nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way.
The Milky Way and Andromeda are expected to collide/merge in about 4–5 billion years.
A nebula is a cloud of gas and dust; some are stellar nurseries where new stars form.
The Orion Nebula (M42) is a nearby, visible star-forming region.
Stars shine by fusing lighter elements into heavier ones in their cores.
A star’s color indicates its surface temperature (blue = hotter, red = cooler).
Red giants are evolved stars that have exhausted core hydrogen and expanded.
White dwarfs are stellar remnants about Earth-sized but with ~Sun-like mass.
The Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) is the maximum mass for a stable white dwarf.
Neutron stars are even denser; a teaspoon would weigh billions of tons on Earth.
Pulsars are rapidly rotating, magnetized neutron stars that emit beams of radiation.
Magnetars are neutron stars with extraordinarily strong magnetic fields.
Black holes are regions where gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.
The event horizon is the “point of no return” around a black hole.
“Spaghettification” describes how extreme tidal forces near black holes can stretch objects.
Quasars are extremely luminous active galactic nuclei powered by supermassive black holes.
Gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) are among the most energetic explosions observed in the universe.
Type Ia supernovae occur when a white dwarf in a binary system reaches a critical mass and explodes.
Redshift shows that distant galaxies are moving away; the universe is expanding.
The cosmic microwave background (CMB) is the afterglow of the Big Bang, now at ~2.7 K.
The universe is about 13.8 billion years old (best current estimate).
Ordinary matter (atoms) makes up only a small fraction of the universe; the rest is dark matter and dark energy.
Dark matter does not emit light; its presence is inferred from gravity’s effects.
Dark energy is associated with the observed acceleration of the universe’s expansion.
Exoplanets are planets orbiting other stars; thousands have been confirmed.
The first exoplanets around a Sun-like star were confirmed in the 1990s.
Many exoplanets have been found via the transit method (tiny dips in starlight).
The “habitable zone” is the range of distances where liquid water could exist on a planet’s surface.
Some exoplanets are “hot Jupiters,” gas giants orbiting very close to their stars.
Europa (a moon of Jupiter) likely has a subsurface ocean beneath an icy shell.
Enceladus (a moon of Saturn) has geysers that eject water-rich plumes into space.
Titan (a moon of Saturn) has lakes and seas of liquid methane and ethane.
Io (a moon of Jupiter) is the most volcanically active body in the solar system.
The Sun will eventually exhaust its core hydrogen and become a red giant in ~5 billion years.
The Sun will not become a supernova; it will shed its outer layers and leave a white dwarf.
A planetary nebula is the glowing shell of gas shed by a dying Sun-like star.
Cosmic rays are high-energy particles from space that can affect electronics and biology.
Space suits provide pressure, oxygen, and temperature control to keep astronauts alive in vacuum.
Microgravity causes muscle and bone loss; astronauts exercise daily to mitigate it.
Spacecraft electronics are “radiation-hardened” to survive harsh space environments.
Constellations are officially defined sky regions; the IAU recognizes 88 of them.
Polaris (the North Star) won’t be the pole star forever due to Earth’s axial precession (~26,000-year cycle).
Annual meteor showers (like the Perseids and Geminids) occur when Earth passes through comet debris.
Saturn’s moon Mimas looks like the “Death Star” due to its large Herschel crater.
The order of the classical planets from the Sun is: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.
